metadata.summary: Production Node.js HTTP APIs with Express or Fastify: middleware stack, validation, error handling, graceful shutdown. Choose Express for ecosystem; Fastify for performance and TypeScript-first.

content.title: Express / Fastify Patterns
content.summary: A methodology for building production Node.js HTTP APIs with Express or Fastify: structured middleware stack (helmet, cors, rate-limit, pino), centralized error handler, schema-validated routes (Zod for Express, TypeBox for Fastify), JWT auth decorator, and graceful shutdown on SIGTERM/SIGINT. Choose Express for ecosystem breadth; Fastify for high-throughput TypeScript-first JSON APIs.

why.p: Express's implicit middleware order and lack of built-in validation cause silent security gaps and inconsistent error shapes. Fastify's plugin encapsulation and schema-first design prevent these but introduce non-obvious scoping rules. Both frameworks require a deliberate middleware stack to be production-safe — missing any one piece (rate-limit, error handler, graceful shutdown) creates either reliability or security issues that only manifest under load or during deploys.

list.item: Bootstrapping a Node.js HTTP API needing a known-good middleware stack.
Choosing between Express (ecosystem) and Fastify (performance, schema, TS-first).
Migrating an Express app to TypeScript-strict + Zod or Fastify + TypeBox.
Greenfield service with fewer than 10 routes where full Nest/Adonis is overkill.
Designing route, error-handler, and auth boundaries an agent can extend safely.
Heavy realtime / streaming workloads — uWebSockets or Go service is cheaper.
Edge-runtime targets (Cloudflare Workers, Vercel Edge) — use Hono or itty-router.
Frontend-only / full-stack frameworks (Next.js Route Handlers, Remix, SvelteKit).
Codebases already standardized on Nest or tRPC — mixing is double-tooling.
Python or Go shops without Node expertise — unnecessary runtime to operate.

how-to.p: Apply this 9-step pipeline to every new Node HTTP API or to harden an existing one. Stop at any step that is already done; never skip a step. The order matters — security middleware before parsing, validation before handlers, graceful shutdown last.
how-to.step: S1 — Pick the framework. Default to Fastify for new TypeScript JSON APIs (>10k req/s ceiling, TypeBox = types + validation + OpenAPI). Pick Express only when the team must reuse passport strategies, legacy middleware, or an existing Express codebase. Record the choice in `.aidocs/decisions/YYYY-MM-DD-framework.md`.

S2 — Scaffold the project layout. Create `src/{app.ts,server.ts,routes/,middleware/,services/,schemas/,plugins/}`, `tests/`, `Dockerfile`, `.env.example`. Pin Node ≥20 LTS in `package.json#engines`. Pin Fastify ecosystem versions exactly (no `^`) — `@fastify/*` minor releases occasionally rename hooks.

S3 — Validate config at startup. Build a Zod schema for `process.env` (PORT, NODE_ENV, DATABASE_URL, JWT_SECRET ≥32 chars, CORS_ORIGINS). Call `EnvSchema.parse(process.env)` before constructing the app. Crash fast on missing/weak config — never default a JWT secret.

S4 — Wire the middleware stack in this exact order (Express): `trust proxy` → `helmet` → `cors` → `compression` → `express.json({ limit })` → `pino-http` (with redact) → `rate-limit` → routes → `notFoundHandler` → `errorHandler` last. For Fastify: register `@fastify/helmet`, `@fastify/cors`, `@fastify/compress`, `@fastify/rate-limit`, `@fastify/sensible`; set `requestIdHeader: 'x-request-id'`; use the built-in error handler with custom mapping.

S5 — Define schemas at module scope, never inside the handler. Use Zod for Express (`CreateUserSchema = z.object({...})`); use TypeBox for Fastify (`Type.Object({...})`). Reject any handler PR that accepts `req.body` without parsing it through a schema first.

S6 — Centralize errors. Create `AppError` base class with subclasses (`ValidationError`, `NotFoundError`, `UnauthorizedError`, `ForbiddenError`, `ConflictError`). One handler maps them to `{ error: { code, message, details? } }`. In Express 4, wrap async handlers with `asyncHandler(fn)` or import `express-async-errors` once at top of `app.ts`. In Fastify, throw `app.httpErrors.*` from `@fastify/sensible`.

S7 — Encapsulate auth. Fastify: register the auth plugin with `fastify-plugin` (fp) wrapper so `app.authenticate` is visible to sibling routes. Express: build an `authenticate` middleware that extracts `Bearer TOKEN`, verifies, and attaches `req.user`. Forbid bypasses gated only by `NODE_ENV` — agents leak these to prod.

S8 — Implement graceful shutdown. Listen for SIGTERM and SIGINT. Sequence: stop accepting new connections (`server.close()` / `app.close()`) → drain in-flight (await close callback) → close DB pools, Redis, queues → `process.exit(0)`. Add a 30s safety timeout that calls `process.exit(1)` if drain hangs.

S9 — Add tests + benchmarks + observability. Vitest + supertest (Express) or `app.inject()` (Fastify) for routes. Run `autocannon -c 100 -d 30 http://localhost:3000/health` to validate the perf floor. Wire OpenTelemetry or Sentry; emit one structured log per request with reqId and userId.

rule.statement: Register middleware in this exact order: security (helmet) → cors → body parsing → logging → rate-limit → routes → 404 handler → error handler. The error handler must be last.
rule.rationale: Express middleware executes in registration order. Placing the error handler before routes means it never receives route errors. Placing rate-limit after logging means log entries exist for blocked requests without rate-limit headers.

rule.statement: Never register errorHandler before routes. An error handler with 4 arguments (err, req, res, next) is only invoked when a route calls next(err).
rule.rationale: Express identifies error handlers by arity (4 arguments). Registering before routes means route-thrown errors fall through to Express's default handler, which returns HTML error pages.

rule.statement: Every route that accepts a request body, params, or query must validate against a schema. Use Zod for Express; TypeBox for Fastify.
rule.rationale: Without validation, malformed input reaches business logic and produces unpredictable behavior. Schema validation also generates a single source of truth for both TypeScript types and runtime checks.

rule.statement: In Fastify, compile schemas at module scope, not inside the handler registration. Define TypeBox schemas once per module.
rule.rationale: Defining schemas inside the handler causes Fastify to recompile the Ajv schema on every request, tanking throughput. Module-scope definition compiles once at startup.

rule.statement: All errors must flow to the centralized error handler via next(err) (Express) or by throwing (Fastify). Never call res.status().json() with an error shape inside a handler.
rule.rationale: Per-handler error responses create inconsistent shapes. One centralized handler enforces one shape, one content-type, and one logging strategy across all endpoints.

rule.statement: In Express 4, wrap async handlers with try/catch and call next(err) in the catch block. In Express 5+, unhandled promise rejections propagate automatically.
rule.rationale: Express 4 does not catch rejected promises from async route handlers. Forgotten next(err) leaves the response hanging indefinitely.

rule.statement: Wrap any plugin that decorates the Fastify instance with fastify-plugin (fp) when the decorator must be visible outside the plugin's scope.
rule.rationale: Fastify's encapsulation model isolates plugins by default. A decorator registered inside a plain plugin is invisible to sibling routes. fastify-plugin opts out of encapsulation for that specific plugin.

rule.statement: Listen for SIGTERM and SIGINT. On signal: stop accepting new connections, drain in-flight requests via server.close(), close DB pools, then exit.
rule.rationale: Container orchestrators (Kubernetes, Docker Compose) send SIGTERM and give the process 10-30 seconds to drain. Without graceful shutdown, mid-request termination causes client errors and data corruption.

checklist.check: Framework choice recorded in `.aidocs/decisions/` with rationale (Express ecosystem vs Fastify perf/TS).
`package.json#engines.node` pinned to `>=20 <23`; Fastify-ecosystem deps pinned exactly (no `^` or `~`).
Env validated at startup with Zod; missing/weak vars crash the process before `app.listen` is called.
JWT_SECRET is ≥32 chars or RS256 with KMS-backed keys; `grep -RIn "JWT_SECRET" src/ tests/ | grep -v process.env` returns nothing.
helmet, cors (explicit origin list, never `*` with `credentials: true`), rate-limit, pino-http (with redact list), compression all registered before routes.
Error handler is the LAST middleware in Express (`app.use(errorHandler)` after all routes); registered via `setErrorHandler` in Fastify.

Every route handler references a schema. `grep -RIn "\.\(get\|post\|put\|patch\|delete\)\(" src/routes/` shows a `schema:` (Fastify) or `validate(...)` (Express+Zod) within 600 chars of the verb call.
No `res.status(...).json(...)` for error shapes inside any handler — all errors flow to the central handler via `next(err)` or `throw`.
Express async handlers either use `express-async-errors` (top-of-app import) or `asyncHandler(fn)` wrapper; no bare `async (req, res) => ...` without one.
Fastify auth/decorator plugins wrapped with `fastify-plugin` (fp) — `grep -RIn "decorate\(" src/plugins/` files all `import fp from 'fastify-plugin'`.
SIGTERM and SIGINT handlers call `server.close()` (Express) or `app.close()` (Fastify), drain DB pools, then `process.exit(0)`; 30s timeout calls `process.exit(1)` as fallback.
`trust proxy` set to `1` (or proxy depth) when behind nginx/ALB; otherwise `req.ip` and rate-limit see the proxy IP.
CORS `origin` is an explicit array or function, never `'*'` together with `credentials: true`.
Hardcoded ports/URLs forbidden — `grep -RIn "localhost:[0-9]" src/` and `grep -RIn "= '3000'" src/` return nothing in route or app code.
`tests/` covers: 200 happy path, 400 schema rejection, 401 missing/invalid token, 403 forbidden role, 500 mapped to error shape, graceful-shutdown does not 502 in-flight.
`autocannon -c 100 -d 30 http://localhost:PORT/health` reports zero non-2xx and ≥5k req/s on a single core.
OpenAPI generated for Fastify via `@fastify/swagger` and committed; `swagger-cli validate openapi.json` passes.

commands.command: pnpm add fastify @fastify/helmet @fastify/cors @fastify/rate-limit @fastify/sensible @fastify/swagger @fastify/swagger-ui @fastify/jwt @sinclair/typebox fastify-plugin pino pino-pretty
pnpm add express helmet cors compression express-rate-limit pino pino-http zod express-async-errors jsonwebtoken && pnpm add -D @types/express @types/cors @types/compression @types/jsonwebtoken
pnpm add -D typescript tsx tsup vitest supertest @vitest/coverage-v8 autocannon eslint @typescript-eslint/parser @typescript-eslint/eslint-plugin eslint-plugin-security eslint-plugin-n
grep -RInE "res\.status\([0-9]+\)\.json\(\s*\{\s*(error|message)" src/routes/ src/controllers/ && echo "FAIL: per-handler error shape" || echo "OK: errors centralized"
grep -RInE "origin:\s*['\"]\*['\"].*credentials.*true|credentials:\s*true.*origin:\s*['\"]\*['\"]" src/ && echo "FAIL: cors wildcard + credentials" || echo "OK"

node -e "const fs=require('fs'),p=require('path'); const dir='src/plugins'; if(!fs.existsSync(dir))process.exit(0); for(const f of fs.readdirSync(dir).filter(x=>/\.ts$/.test(x))){const s=fs.readFileSync(p.join(dir,f),'utf8'); if(/decorate\(/.test(s) && !/fastify-plugin/.test(s)){console.error('FAIL',f,'decorates without fp wrapper');process.exit(1)}} console.log('OK: all plugins use fp')"

grep -RInE "app\.(get|post|put|patch|delete)\([^)]*async" src/routes/ | grep -v "asyncHandler\|express-async-errors" && echo "WARN: bare async handlers, ensure express-async-errors imported in app.ts" || echo "OK"
grep -RInE "(jwt\.sign|jwt\.verify)\(.*['\"][A-Za-z0-9]{1,31}['\"]" src/ && echo "FAIL: weak/literal JWT secret" || echo "OK"

docker run --rm -d --name api-shutdown-test myapi:latest && sleep 2 && docker stop --time=30 api-shutdown-test && docker logs api-shutdown-test | grep -E "SIGTERM received|HTTP server closed" || echo "FAIL: graceful shutdown not logged"

example.title: Express application setup
example.note: Middleware order is explicit. errorHandler registered last. Rate-limit after logging. `express-async-errors` imported at the very top so async-rejection propagation is in effect for every subsequent file.

example.title: Express graceful shutdown
example.note: Forced exit after 30 seconds prevents hanging if connections don't drain. `.unref()` so the timer itself does not keep the event loop alive.

example.title: Fastify TypeBox route
example.note: TypeBox schema is the source of truth for both runtime validation and TypeScript types. Handler is thin: bind → service → return. Schemas are defined at module scope, not inside the handler — Fastify compiles them once at startup.

example.title: Fastify auth plugin
example.note: fp() wrapper is mandatory for the decorator to be visible to routes registered in sibling plugins. Without it, `app.authenticate` is `undefined` outside this plugin's scope and the failure is silent at registration time.

example.title: Express centralized error handler with AppError hierarchy
example.note: One handler, one shape. Zod errors are mapped to ValidationError automatically, so route handlers can call `Schema.parse(req.body)` and let the rejection propagate via express-async-errors.

verify.step: V1 — `pnpm tsc --noEmit` passes with no errors.

V3 — `pnpm vitest run --coverage` shows ≥80% line coverage on `src/routes/` and `src/services/`; tests cover at minimum: 200 happy path, 400 schema rejection, 401 missing/invalid token, 500 mapped error shape.
V4 — `bash templates/audit-routes.sh src/routes` exits 0; every verb has a schema, mutating verbs have auth/preHandler, file references error wiring.
V5 — All `grep-*` commands in `<commands>` print only `OK:`; none print `FAIL:` or match anti-patterns.
V6 — `npx autocannon -c 100 -d 30 http://localhost:3000/health` shows zero non-2xx and ≥5k req/s on a single core; latency p99 <50ms.

V7 — Graceful shutdown: start the process, send SIGTERM, send a request mid-shutdown — the request must complete with 200 and the next must get connection-refused. Verify via `docker stop --time=30 <container>` and check logs for `HTTP server closed` before exit.
V8 — Inspect a structured log line: it MUST contain `reqId`, `userId` (when authenticated), `method`, `url`, `statusCode`, `responseTime`. Authorization headers and password fields MUST NOT appear.

V9 — For Fastify only: `node -e "const f=require('fastify')(); f.register(require('./dist/plugins/auth')); f.ready().then(()=>console.log(typeof f.authenticate))"` prints `function`. If it prints `undefined`, the plugin is missing the `fp` wrapper.
V10 — OpenAPI: `swagger-cli validate openapi.json` passes; the spec covers every public route.

content.prompt:
You are a Node.js HTTP route-builder agent. Generate a single resource route module.
Inputs: - framework: "fastify" | "express" - resource_name: <kebab-case singular> - operations: list of {verb, path, auth_required, body_schema, response_schema} - service_module: path to the service module that already exists
Hard rules: 1. Schemas MUST be declared at module scope (TypeBox for Fastify, Zod for Express). Never inside the handler. 2. Every mutating verb (POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE) MUST have auth: `preHandler: [app.authenticate]` (Fastify) or `authenticate` middleware (Express). 3. Errors MUST propagate via `throw app.httpErrors.*` (Fastify) or `next(err)` / `throw` with express-async-errors (Express). NEVER `res.status(...).json({ error })` inside the handler. 4. Handler body is thin: validate (auto via schema) → call service → return result. Max 15 lines per handler. 5. Output exactly one TypeScript file. No prose, no markdown, no triple-backtick fences. 6. Include `import` statements; assume `service_module` exports its functions named `<verb><Resource>`. 7. For Fastify: export a `FastifyPluginAsync`. Do NOT wrap with `fp` (routes are encapsulated by design). 8. For Express: export a `Router`. Wrap async with `asyncHandler` only if `express-async-errors` is not the team default.
Output format: a single .ts file body, ready to drop into src/routes/<resource_name>.ts.

You are reviewing one Node HTTP route file for production readiness. Output a JSON object — no prose:
{ "framework": "express|fastify|unknown", "issues": [ {"severity": "error|warning", "rule": "<short slug>", "line": <int>, "fix": "<imperative one-liner>"} ], "ok": <bool> // true iff no errors }
Rules to check (severity error unless noted): - E-SCHEMA : every verb call has a `schema:` (Fastify) or zod `parse`/`safeParse` (Express) - E-AUTH : POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE has preHandler/authenticate - E-ERROR-LEAK : no `res.status(>=400).json({ error })` inside handler — must propagate - E-ASYNC-WRAP : async Express handlers either inside express-async-errors scope or wrapped - E-FP-MISSING : Fastify plugin uses `decorate()` but is NOT wrapped with fp() (warning if file is in routes/, error if in plugins/) - E-CORS-STAR : cors origin: '*' with credentials:true - E-JWT-LITERAL : jwt.sign / jwt.verify with a literal string secret - W-SCHEMA-INLINE: schema object literal is INSIDE the handler registration (warning, perf) - W-RES-STATUS : res.status used after `next(err)` was called (warning, dead code)
Output JSON only, sorted by line number.

antipattern.description: Callback-based async handler in Express: User.findById(id, (err, user) => {...}).
antipattern.reason: Callback-based code cannot use try/catch for error propagation. Errors require explicit res.status(500) calls in every callback, creating inconsistent shapes and missed error paths. Use async/await with next(err) in catch.

antipattern.description: Async Express handler without try/catch and next(err): async (req, res) => { const u = await find(); res.json(u); }.
antipattern.reason: Express 4 does not catch rejected promises from async handlers. If find() rejects, the response hangs indefinitely. Wrap in try/catch and call next(err), or use Express 5 / express-async-errors.

antipattern.description: Route handler without a schema in Fastify or validate() middleware in Express.
antipattern.reason: Unvalidated request bodies reach business logic and databases with arbitrary shapes. Schema validation is the only reliable defense against malformed input.

antipattern.description: CORS configured with origin: '*' and credentials: true.
antipattern.reason: This combination is invalid per the CORS spec and causes a browser error at runtime. Browsers block credentialed cross-origin requests when the server responds with a wildcard origin.

antipattern.description: Hardcoded port, JWT secret, or database URL as string literals in application code.
antipattern.reason: Secrets committed to source or bundled into images are leaked to anyone with repository or container access. Use process.env and validate at startup; reject missing required config.

antipattern.description: Fastify auth decorator registered in a plain plugin (not wrapped with fastify-plugin).
antipattern.reason: Fastify's encapsulation isolates decorators to the plugin's scope. Routes outside the plugin cannot call app.authenticate. The failure is silent — the decorator simply does not exist on the outer instance.

antipattern.description: Logging raw req.body or req.headers without redacting authorization, cookie, and password fields.
antipattern.reason: Production logs containing credentials create audit failures and enable credential theft via log aggregation systems. Use pino's redact option to strip sensitive fields before logging.

list.item: Pin versions exactly (package.json no carets) for the Fastify ecosystem; minor releases of @fastify/* plugins occasionally rename hooks.
Use fastify-plugin (fp) for any decorator that must escape encapsulation — forgetting this is the #1 Fastify bug agents introduce.
Centralize error class hierarchy (AppError → ValidationError, NotFoundError, UnauthorizedError) and map them in one error handler. Don't res.status(...).json(...) in handlers.
Validate at the edge with the same schema you use for types. Zod (Express) or TypeBox (Fastify) gives one source of truth; never hand-write a TypeScript interface for a payload.
Log a request ID + user ID with every line via pino-http genReqId or Fastify's requestIdHeader; without it, prod debugging is impossible.
Graceful shutdown is non-negotiable. Listen for SIGTERM, stop accepting new connections, drain in-flight, close DB pools, then exit. Container orchestrators give you 10-30s — use them.
Set app.set('trust proxy', 1) in Express behind a single proxy (Nginx, ALB); without it req.ip and rate-limit see the proxy IP only.
Guard secrets at boundary. Never log req.body or req.headers raw; redact authorization, cookie, password via Pino's redact option.
Use @fastify/sensible for httpErrors rather than throwing raw Error; status codes stay consistent without per-route boilerplate.
Compile schemas once. In Fastify, define schemas at module scope; redefining inside the handler tanks throughput.

section.name: framework-choice
section.p: Choose Express when: team has existing Express/middleware knowledge, you need maximum ecosystem breadth (e.g., passport strategies), or the codebase is already Express. Choose Fastify when: building a new TypeScript-first JSON API, throughput matters (>10k req/s per core), or you value schema-as-contract (TypeBox → types + validation + OpenAPI). Both are suitable for cloud-native deployments; Fastify edges out Express in cold-start latency.

list.item: Express's middleware order is implicit. Reorder one app.use and 4xx/5xx behavior changes silently. Newer engineers (and LLMs) misplace errorHandler ahead of routes.
No built-in validation in Express. Without zod / joi the API ships unvalidated bodies; error shape becomes a per-route snowflake.
Async error swallowing. Forgetting next(err) in an async Express handler hangs the response. Express 5 fixes most cases; Express 4 still bites.

Fastify schema lock-in. TypeBox / Ajv schemas double as runtime validators and TS types — great until you need a dynamic schema; serialization shortcuts can also drop fields silently if the response schema omits them.
Plugin scoping in Fastify. app.register creates encapsulation contexts; decorators leak in non-obvious ways. LLMs frequently forget fastify-plugin wrappers.
Performance ceiling. Both frameworks plateau around 30-80k req/s on a single core; CPU-heavy work (JSON parsing, JWT verify) dominates. Don't expect a framework swap to fix architectural bottlenecks.
Ecosystem rot. Express middlewares often unmaintained (body-parser, morgan, custom CSRF). Audit before adopting; many have CVEs or have been superseded.

LLMs love deprecated Express middleware. Models still emit body-parser, morgan, cookie-parser separately even though express.json() and structured pino replace them. Pin the prompt to "Express ≥4.18, no body-parser import."
Async error handlers in Express 4. Agents forget next(err) in async functions; use express-async-errors or wrap with asyncHandler. Express 5 mostly fixes this — confirm the version.

Fastify plugin encapsulation. Agents place auth decorators in a normal plugin (encapsulated) and then can't use them in sibling routes. Force-prompt: "wrap with fastify-plugin if it must be visible outside this scope."
Schema-less agent code. Without explicit schemas, generated routes pass any body. Reject any handler PR without schema (Fastify) or validate(...) middleware (Express + Zod).
Hardcoded ports / origins. Agents inline 3000 and http://localhost:3000; force config via process.env and reject string literals in route or app code.
CORS wildcard. Default LLM output is cors({ origin: '*' }) with credentials: true — that's a runtime error in modern browsers. Specify origins explicitly.
JWT secret length. Agents pick weak HS256 secrets in examples; reject anything less than 32 chars in JWT_SECRET or push to RS256 with KMS-backed keys.
Logging the request body. Pino's default with pino-http may include req.body; agents forget to enable redaction. Mandate the redact list.
Graceful shutdown swallowed. Agents register SIGTERM but don't wait for server.close()'s callback; container kills mid-request. Verify the close→exit chain in review.
Test-only auth bypass leaking. Agents add if (process.env.NODE_ENV === 'test') skip auth and forget the env check on prod; treat as a security gate, not a convenience.
Streaming responses break Fastify schema serialization. Agents wire response: { 200: SomeSchema } then return a stream — Fastify drops the body. Disable response schema for streamed routes.
pnpm / npm / bun — Package management; pnpm recommended for monorepos
tsx — Run TypeScript without compile step (dev/watch)
tsup / esbuild — Production bundle to single ESM file
pino-pretty — Local log formatter for pino JSON logs
vitest — Test runner; works with both frameworks; supports fastify.inject and supertest
supertest / @fastify/inject — HTTP integration testing
autocannon — Built-in benchmarking (Fastify devs maintain it)
clinic.js — Node.js performance profiling (flame, doctor, bubbleprof)
eslint --fix + @typescript-eslint — Static analysis; pair with eslint-plugin-security and eslint-plugin-node
npm-check-updates (ncu) — Detect outdated middleware / plugin versions
swagger-cli / redocly — Validate / preview the generated OpenAPI from @fastify/swagger
@fastify/swagger + @fastify/swagger-ui — OSS plugin. Auto-generates OpenAPI from TypeBox schemas — excellent agent input/output contract.
Stoplight / Redocly — SaaS / OSS. Render OpenAPI as docs portal; CLI-driven.
Sentry / Highlight — SaaS APM. Both frameworks have first-class SDKs; agents can wire @sentry/node in less than 10 lines.
Datadog APM / OpenTelemetry SDK — SaaS / OSS. OTel auto-instruments Express + Fastify; preferred for vendor-neutral telemetry.
Better Stack / Logtail — SaaS log + uptime. Pino transport ships JSON logs directly.
Render / Railway / Fly.io / Hetzner — PaaS / IaaS. All deploy a Node Dockerfile in one command — match to budget.
Cloudflare Workers / Vercel Edge — Serverless. No for Express. Use Hono instead; Fastify v5 has partial Edge support but not recommended.
AWS Lambda + @fastify/aws-lambda / serverless-http — SaaS. Cold-start tax; only worth it for spiky traffic.
PM2 — OSS process manager. Cluster mode + zero-downtime reload; less needed if already in Docker/K8s.
Auth0 / Clerk / Supabase Auth — SaaS auth. Drop-in JWT issuer; pair with the Fastify decorator pattern.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Drive Node API work as a 4-stage pipeline. (1) An architect agent reads the README + existing repo and decides Express vs Fastify, validation lib (Zod vs TypeBox), and folder layout. (2) A scaffolder agent (haiku-class sufficient) emits app.ts, server.ts, routes/, middleware/, and a Dockerfile from templates. (3) A route-builder agent generates handlers, schemas, and tests one resource at a time, gated by contract spec (OpenAPI or TypeBox source-of-truth). (4) A reviewer / SDD agent verifies error handling, logging context, auth scoping, and graceful shutdown before merge. Persist decisions to .aidocs/decisions/ so subsequent agents stay consistent.

template.purpose: Script: flag route handlers missing schema, auth, or error wiring
